coredata.dc:title: Preparation and evaluation of a poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) derived graphene quantum dots based hydrophilic interaction and reversed-phase mixed-mode stationary phase for complex sample analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Qiurong
Zhong, Zhirong
Zheng, Yunchao
Gao, Die
Xia, Zhining
Wang, Lujun
coredata.dc:description: The poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (NIPAAm) was first polymerized onto the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) functionalized silica as packing materials via reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization reaction, which can expand the interaction modes between stationary phase and analytes. A series of characteristic methods were selected to estimate the chemical bonding results of silica, involving Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), elemental analysis (EA), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and scanning electron microscope (SEM). The prepared column can exhibit reversed-phase and hydrophilic interaction modes, which were demonstrated by the retention of eight kinds of target analytes with different Log P values. The column was then applied to separate banlangen granules and further verified by HPLC tandem time-of-flight mass spectrometry (HPLC/QTOF-MS). In conclusion, Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm stationary phase improved the analysis range and performance of traditional phases, exhibiting flexible selectivity and application prospect for both hydrophobic and hydrophilic analytes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Poly(N-isopropylacrylamide)
Mixed-mode stationary phase
Reversed-phase/hydrophilic interaction
Chromatographic separation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of DMP, GQDs and Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm stationary phase
Effect of mobile phase proportion on analyte retention
Determination of hydrophilic ingredients in banlangen granules

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Department of Science and Technology of Sichuan Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Education of Sichuan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 21804112), the Project of Department of Education of Sichuan Province (NO. 18ZA0527), the talent introduction program of Southwest Medical University (NO. 090300040040), the university level fund of Southwest Medical University (NO. 2017-ZRQN-0118) and the Sichuan Science and Technology Program (2020YFS0313).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for synthesis of Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm stationary phase.
FT-IR spectra of silica, Sil-APTES, Sil-GQDs-DMP and Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm (a) and Thermogravimetric curves of silica, Sil-APTES, Sil-GQDs, Sil-GQDs-DMP and Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm (b).

Separation of (1) thiourea, (2) thymidine, (3) deoxyadenosine, (4) adenosine, (5) uridine, (6) cytosine and (7) cytidine with Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm column. Retentions for varying trend diagram of versus nucleosides/nucleobases the water proportion (b). Conditions: mobile phase: ACN/water (87:13, v/v). UV detection: 254 nm.

Separation of (1) toluene, (2) ethylbenzene, (3) N-propylbenzene, (4) butylbenzene and (5) N-pentylbenzene in chromatogram (a) and (1) naphthalene, (2) acenaphthene, (3) phenanthrene, (4) pyrene and (5) chrysene in chromatogram (c) with Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm column. Retentions for varying trend diagram of alkylbenzenes (b) and PAHs (d) versus the water proportion. Conditions: mobile phase: MeOH/water (45:55, v/v) (a); MeOH/water (62:38, v/v) (c); UV detection: 214 nm (a), 254 nm (c).

Separation of (1) P-phenylenediamine, (2) O-phenylenediamine, (3) biphenyldiamine, (4) 1-naphthylamine, (5) 2,4-dinitroaniline and (6) diphenylamine in chromatogram (a) and (1) hydroquinone, (2) O-nitrophenol and (3) P-nitrophenol in chromatogram (c) with Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm column. Retentions for varying trend diagram of anilines (b) and phenols (d) versus the water proportion. Conditions: mobile phase: MeOH/water (40:60, v/v) (a); MeOH/water (35:65, v/v) (c); UV detection: 254 nm.

Separation of (1)O-terphenyl, (2) M-terphenyl and (3) Triphenylene in chromatogram (a) Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm column. Retentions for varying trend diagram of biphenyls (b) versus the water proportion. Conditions: mobile phase: MeOH/water (65:35, v/v) (a); UV detection: 254 nm.

Chromatogram of banlangen granules and the corresponding standards. Conditions: mobile phase: ACN/water (92:8, v/v) (a); UV detection: 254 nm. Peaks: (1) Uridine, (2) Syringin, (3) Adenosine and (4) Adenine.
Mass spectra of samples in positive mode. Uridine (a); Syringin (b); Adenosine (c); and Adenine (d).
Elemental analysis data of silica, Sil-APTES, Sil-GQDs, Sil-GQDs-DMP and Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm.
Identification of components of banlangen granules with HPLC-QTOF/MS data.

head.ce:title: Preparation and evaluation of a poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) derived graphene quantum dots based hydrophilic interaction and reversed-phase mixed-mode stationary phase for complex sample analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan, 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan, 646000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 401331, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 401331, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Medical Electrophysiology, Ministry of Education, Institute of Cardiovascular Research of Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan, 646000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Cardiovascular Research of Southwest Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Medical Electrophysiology, Ministry of Education, Institute of Cardiovascular Research of Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan, 646000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan, 646000, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Qiurong Luo and Zhirong Zhong contributed equally to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (NIPAAm) was first polymerized onto the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) functionalized silica as packing materials via reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization reaction, which can expand the interaction modes between stationary phase and analytes. A series of characteristic methods were selected to estimate the chemical bonding results of silica, involving Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), elemental analysis (EA), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and scanning electron microscope (SEM). The prepared column can exhibit reversed-phase and hydrophilic interaction modes, which were demonstrated by the retention of eight kinds of target analytes with different Log P values. The column was then applied to separate banlangen granules and further verified by HPLC tandem time-of-flight mass spectrometry (HPLC/QTOF-MS). In conclusion, Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm stationary phase improved the analysis range and performance of traditional phases, exhibiting flexible selectivity and application prospect for both hydrophobic and hydrophilic analytes.

ce:section.ce:para: In the history of separation science, stationary phase was considered as a key factor in promoting the development of chromatographic separation. Silica-based sorbents as one of the most widely used matrix of HPLC stationary phases have been extensively used in scientific research, manufacturing [1], medicine and others [2,3]. Among these applications, the widely used HPLC analytical tool is reversed-phase chromatography (RPLC) used for the separation of medium and low polar compounds [4]. Hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography (HILIC) is chosen for separation of polar compounds [5]. Despite their superior selectivity and broad applicability, the analytical process suffers from major drawbacks, i.e. single mode separation faces some difficulties for analysis of complex samples, such as environment, food and pharmaceuticals. With the development of modern instrumental analysis, mixed-mode chromatography (MMC) has become a complementary and more efficient approach for simultaneous separation of high complex or practical samples. Hitherto, a great deal of MMC have been reported, such as RPLC/HILIC [6,7], RPLC/ion exchange chromatography (IEC) [8,9], RPLC/HILIC/IEC [10,11], etc. With the demand for novel chromatographic stationary phase, there is an urgent need to find more practical multifunctional materials to further extend wide-range feasibility and high separation selectivity of modern HPLC separation technology.

Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are burgeoning nanomaterials derived from graphene oxide (GO) with potential market application value. Especially their exceptional physicochemical properties, such as excellent dispersibility, abundant chemical sites and good biocompatibility [12], which have contributed versatile applications in bioimaging [13,14], energy storage systems [15–17], catalysis [18–20], sensing device [21–23] and nanomedicine diagnostics [24]. Notably, GQDs have attained the attention of the chromatographic analysis in the scientific community especially for their excellent performance in stationary phase. Initially, GQDs were directly covalently bonded to the silica forming a novel HPLC stationary phase and applied in multiple separation modes including normal phase liquid chromatography (NPLC), RPLC and HILIC [25]. Yet deficiencies still existed such as poor peak shape or weak separation capacity and so on. Afterwards, ionic liquid (ILs) [26], octadecyl [27] and chiral ligand [28] modified GQDs-based stationary phases were carried out to acquire better separation performance and wider application range. GQDs can provide hydrophobic, hydrophilic, π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding interactions between stationary phase and analytes confirmed by above-mentioned reports. Within the next few years, GQDs are expected to become potential and promising modified functional nanomaterials for the novel chromatographic stationary phases.

Poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAAm) was a macromolecular polymer with hydrophilic amide and hydrophobic isopropyl groups, which was applied in liquid chromatography stationary phase [29,30] and biomedical investigations [31]. On the basis of the previous studies, NIPAAm can be grafted onto surface of silica via the RAFT polymerization by using of dodecyl trithiocarbonate compounds under mild conditions [32].

Hence, the present study is aimed on the topic of searching preferable functional monomer for column packing and broadening the application range of GQDs-based stationary phase. In the current research, NIPAAm was polymerized on the surface of GQDs-based materials with 2-(((dodecylthio)carbonothioyl)thio) propanoic acid (DMP) as the RAFT chain transfer agent through RAFT polymerization. DMP and amide groups contained in PNIPAAm can endow GQDs-based chromatographic column dual retention mechanisms including HILIC and RPLC modes, which were further tested by a series of polar and non-polar compounds. To reflect the performance of the prepared column, we also did a comparison with three columns. Additional studies to understand the key factor of retention mechanisms for the new column were investigated, containing varying mobile phase content and making Tanaka test. Finally, the application for complicated samples was conducted by analyzing the chemical constituents of banlangen granules.

Silica beads (diameter 5.5 μm, pore size 100 Å, and surface area 465 m2 g−1) were obtained from Daiso Chemicals (Osaka, Japan). The N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAAm, 97%, Tokyo Kasei Kagyo Co., Ltd) was purified by recrystallization from a mixture of toluene and hexane (3/2, v/v). Graphite powder was purchased from Lanmu Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Nicotinamide was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Mono-substituted benzenes, PAHs, Biphenyls, nucleosides/nucleobases, phenols, anilines and amino acids (phenylalanine, tyrosine, tryptophan) used for chromatographic evaluation were obtained from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Vitamin B1 was provided by Innochem Reagent Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Pyridoxine and riboflavin were obtained from Ark Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Thiourea was purchased from General Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Adenine was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). A stainless steel column (4.6 × 150 mm i.d.) was purchased from Dalian Replete Science and Technology Co., Ltd (Dalian, China). The commercial ZORBAX SB-C18 (4.6 × 150 mm i.d., 5 μm) and ZORBAX NH2 (4.6 × 150 mm i.d., 5 μm) columns were provided by Agilent Technologies (California, USA).

Elemental analysis was performed on Vario EL III elemental analyzer (Elementar, Germany). The Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) was carried out on IR Affinity-1S FT-IR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Results of thermogravimetric analysis were obtained on STA 449 F3 system (Netzsch, Germany). UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra were characterized using a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and a LS55 luminescence spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA), respectively. The surface morphologies of particles were observed on a SPURA 55 Field emission scanning electron microscopy (ZEISS, Germany. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was conducted on a Tecnai F30 transmission electron microscope at 200 kV (FEI, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of DMP, GQDs and Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm stationary phase
ce:section.ce:para: DMP as the RAFT chain transfer agent was synthesized according to modified literature procedure [33]. 1H NMR result was in consistent with the reported reference and the graph was shown in Fig. S1.

Natural graphite powders were oxidized to graphene oxide (GO) by using a simplified Hummer’s method. GQDs were prepared from GO aqueous dispersion using a hydrothermal synthesis method with H2O2 as an etching agent and ammonia as an assistant agent according to previously reported method [34]. In addition, the procedure for synthesis of Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm stationary phase was shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, activated silica was prepared based on the preparation experience of our group [35]. The next steps were done by adopting the published experimental method [25]. The detailed procedure and characterization methods including FT-IR, TEM, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were described in Supporting Information.

For packing the Sil-GQDs and Sil-GQDs-PNIPAM columns, the above two materials were dispersed in a mixed solvent of isopropanol/acetonitrile (1/1, v/v) via ultrasonication for 30 s, respectively. The subsequent packing procedures are the same as our previous publication [35]. Besides, the prepared chromatographic column was flushed and activated by methanol overnight on a HPLC instrument.

All the chromatographic analysis was performed on Prominence-I LC-2030 series HPLC system consisting of LC-20AD solvent delivery pump, SIL-20AC autosampler, CTO-20AC column oven and SPD-20AV detector. The chromatographic separation performance of Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm column was compared with commercial C18 and NH2 columns. Separation was proceeded at the column temperature of 35 °C, at a flow rate of 0.8 mL/min.

Initially, the prepared GQDs were confirmed by FT-IR, TEM, UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. FT-IR spectrum of GQDs was shown in Fig. S2, which showed the prepared GQDs were essentially identical to that reported in the literature. A broad band centered around 3153 cm−1 was attributed to H-bond associated with OH. A characteristic peak at 1614 cm−1 was assigned to CO stretching vibrations of the carbonyl and carboxyl groups. C–OH groups were obtained at the band of 1392 cm−1. Additionally, a band at 1118 cm−1 was due to C–O stretching vibrations. In TEM images, GQDs with a homogeneous size (an average size of 2.97 nm) could be obviously observed (Fig. S3). Fig. S4a showed the UV–vis spectra of GO and GQDs, which revealed a peak at 300 nm of strongly blue-shifted by contrast of GO. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of GQDs showed a strong maximum excitation and emission wavelengths appearing at 475 nm and 522 nm (Fig. S4b). And when the excitation wavelength changed from 405 nm to 505 nm, the PL peak shifted from 473 nm to 529 nm, indicating a typical large red-shift phenomenon (from 473 nm to 529 nm) (Fig. S4c). These results showed that the GQDs for preparation of Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm stationary phase were synthesized successfully.

The conjugation of GQDs, DMP and PNIPAAm to the surface of SiO2 was confirmed by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the broad and strong characteristic vibration at 3500 cm−1 on the spectrum of bare SiO2 ascribed to the vibration of the silanol, while the peak became weak after reacting with APTES for the shielding of silanol group. In the spectrum of Sil-GQDs, the broad peak at around 3500 cm−1 was appeared again indicating successful bonding of GQDs. It was notable that absorption peaks at around 2927 and 2858 cm−1 obviously enhanced, which were ascribed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of C–H respectively came from dodecyl chains of CTA. As expected, the appearance of typical bands could testify the covalent attachment of the APTES, GQDs and NIPAAm onto silica. For example, the CO and N–H bonds of the amide groups from Sil-GQDs were observed at around 1433 and 1338 cm−1. Compared with the Sil-GQDs-DMP, the final product exhibited a slightly increased intensity of the amide peak at 1548 and 1467 cm−1 due to the additional CO groups from the PNIPAAm. Based on the above analysis, these results indicated that the GQDs, DMP and NIPAAm were grafted onto SiO2 surface.

In addition, the surface coverage of silica was verified by elemental analysis data (C, H, N, S). As listed in Table 1 , The C content of 13.32% demonstrated a higher increment than silica, and the S content increased from 0.07% to 3.37%, which could prove that DMP have coated onto the silicon surface. Besides, according to the equations described in our previous papers [35], only the surface coverage of APTES, DMP and PNIPAAm could be figured out, aminopropyl groups attached onto the silica was calculated as 4.04 μmol m−2 and PNIPAAm was 1.71 μmol m−2 based on the content of nitrogen. From increment of sulfur content, the graft degree of DMP were 0.74 μmol m−2. The calculation equations were described as follows: Sil-APTES (coverage) =
N % ×
10 6
14 × S
, Sil-GQDs-DMP (coverage) =
S % ×
10 6
3 × 32 × S
, Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm (coverage) =
N % ×
10 6
14 × S
, where S%, and N% represent the percentage of sulfur and nitrogen, respectively, as determined by elemental analysis. S represents the surface area of silica.

After that, TGA experiment was employed to estimate the grafting amounts of immobilized compounds on silica and the thermal stability of materials (Fig. 2b). The weight loss of 5.5% below 120 °C was ascribed to the absorbed water or residual solvent on the surface of silica. Especially, Sil-GQDs with decomposition temperature higher than Sil-APTES afforded two consecutive steps of weight loss at 25 and 180 °C, while Sil-GQDs showed three consecutive steps of weight loss at 25, 120 and 360 °C. These results suggested that the bonding of GQDs might help to improve the thermal stability of the material. Compared with Sil-GQDs, the thermogravimetric curve trend of Sil-GQDs-DMP indicated a much steeper decrease obviously and the mass loss ratio was up to 24.2% with the increase of temperature, which can be inclined to massive loss of organic ligand grafted on the surface. Nevertheless, the amount of polymer PNIPAAm modified onto silica was less than 1.5% according to mass loss, and the data was in consistent with the immobilized amounts estimated from the elemental analysis.

The morphology of the spherical silica gel can change as a result of modification. Therefore, the stationary phase was further identified by SEM. Compared to glossy surface of bare silica (Fig. S5a), some small clusters appeared onto the surface of Sil-GQDs while whose surface still kept relatively smooth in Fig. S5b. From the Fig. S5c, the morphology of final materials became evidently more scabrous after polymerization. As a result, this piece of evidence further proved that the success of the prepared Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm packing materials.

The hydrophilic moieties including GQDs ligands, neutral hydrophilic groups (e.g. hydroxyl and amide) may contribute to water-rich layer formation on the surface of stationary phase in methanol-rich mobile phase. And Log P values can reflect the hydrophilicity of target analytes (Table S1). Three types of hydrophilic compounds were chosen as probes to evaluate the separation performance of the Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm column in HILIC mode and compared with the Sil-GQDs column. Fig. 3 and Fig. S6 showed that these three types of hydrophilic solutes maintain a certain level of retention as the mobile phase flowed through Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm column. That can be ascribed to the existence of hydrophilic groups (GQDs groups and amide groups) in final packing materials, resulting in strong hydrophilic chromatographic performance. For those compounds with a negative Log P value like vitamins and nucleotides/nucleobases, exhibited better separation capabilities for hydrophilic analytes on the column of Sil-GQDs (Fig. S7a and Fig. S7b). The reason may be attributed to lack of hydrophobic dodecyl chains, more likely to a HILIC column. Yet tyrosine and tryptophan with similar chemical structures couldn’t be effectively separated on column of Sil-GQDs but achieve separation on the Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm stationary phase under the same chromatographic condition. Although these three amino acids get a separation in the optimal condition but only with worse peak shape (Fig. S7c).

Specifically, it’s essential to perform further analysis on chromatographic behavior of each class of representative compounds. As shown in Fig. 3a, seven nucleosides/nucleobases separated successfully while their elute order was not inclined with related Log P value. It partly attributed to that either hydrophobic interaction from dodecyl chain or π conjugative systems of GQDs caused the formation of π-π interaction, of course, as well as the role of hydrogen-bond interaction. Compared with NH2 column in Fig. S8, the peaks order made a difference and the phenomena of peaks tailing were greatly improved from the tailing factors of uridine (As = 1.893 for NH2 column and As = 0.995 for the prepared column), adenosine (As = 2.569 for NH2 column and As = 1.066 for the prepared column) and cytidine (As = 1.855 for NH2 column and As = 1.030 for the prepared column). So, the separation of nucleosides not just result from hydrophilic interaction but comprehensive mechanism. Beyond that, other two probes were adopted to assess the separating effect under HILIC mode of this prepared column. The chromatograms were displayed in Fig. S6. Chromatographic behaviors of vitamins and amino acids were also compared with NH2 column (Fig. S9). Four water-solubility vitamins were separated in 10 min and obtain better resolution. And it was shown that along with the increase of the values of Log P, the retentions became stronger. Surprisingly, a change in peak order of riboflavin and pyridoxine may attribute to lack of π-π interaction on NH2 column. For weak hydrophilic amino acids, the peak order in HPLC curve was not agreement with corresponding Log P values. And this can be ascribed to the number of hydrogen bonds formed and π conjugation degree.

As is known to all, the GQDs possess a powerful π-π conjugate system and DMP as the RAFT chain transfer agent, both of them endow the Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm column RPLC selectivity to exhibit hydrophobic and π-π interactions with hydrophobic compounds. Log P values of corresponding analytes were listed in Table S1. As depicted in Fig. S7d and Fig. S10, compared with Sil-GQDs column, these analytes could achieve complete separation and have the higher retention strength. Even though in the optimal condition, only the alkylbenzenes and PAHs could be barely separated but the results were bad for the peak tailing. The explanation for this chromatographic behavior was also due to multiple interactions of π-π interaction, hydrophobic and hydrogen-bond interactions provided with the prepared chromatographic stationary phase.

Likewise, a further discussion concerned with each chromatogram of benzene and its derivatives would be described in more detailed below. As shown in Fig. 4 a, Figs. 4c and 5a , the three kinds of analytes (alkylbenzenes, PAHs and anilines) were eluted in accordance with the order of their hydrophobicity. In addition, this new column exhibited an enhanced ability to recognize molecular planarity, which may be attributed to the effect of the DMP and PNIPAAm groups compared with Sil-GQDs column. Moreover, these hydrophobic analytes were also separated with commercial C18 column but needed longer time for complete separation such as PAHs (Fig. S10a). And it was observed that the selectivity for O-terphenyl and M-terphenyl was evidently higher on the Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm column than on the Sil-GQDs column from Fig. 6 . Even under the optimal condition, the first three amine compounds or M-terphenyl and triphenylene couldn’t achieve separation with C18 from Fig. S11b and Fig. S11c, which indicated that the mixed-mode chromatographic column had the feature of steric selectivity. Noteworthily, the phenomenon of triphenylene keeping a stronger retention than M-terphenyl with higher Log P, probably contributed to more powerful π electron conjugation system in structure of triphenylene. In a similar above, the abnormal behavior was also appeared in result of phenols, especially O-nitrophenol and P-nitrophenol while the peak order was consistent with their Log P values on commercial C18 column in Fig. S11d. It was well known that there were strong intra-molecular hydrogen bonds in O-nitrophenol leading to weaken the force of hydrogen bonds between the stationary phase. Hence, the retention of P-nitrophenol was longer compare with O-nitrophenol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of mobile phase proportion on analyte retention
ce:section.ce:para: To get an in-depth understanding of the retention mechanism of Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm column, the varies of the volume-fraction of organic phase in the mobile phase with the logarithm of the retention factor of selected measurement were investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 4b, d, Fig. 5b, d and 6b, the retentions (log k) weakened with the increase of organic proportion in mobile phase, indicating that this column exhibited a typical RPLC retention characteristic. Particularly worth mentioning is that the retention of three phenols with content of methanol presented good linearity, which was in accordance with an adsorptive process caused by the decreased amount of soluble polar sites in solutes. As for other four substituted benzenes, the chromatograms displayed discontinuous retention patterns due to a liquid-liquid partitioning behavior [36]. In addition, curve chart of log k with nucleosides/nucleobases and amino acids upon an increase of organic phase content of the mobile phase was also investigated (Fig. 3b and Fig. S6b), and the whole curve performed an increased trend, indicating that these hydrophilic analytes followed the HILIC separation mode.

Especially, a U-shaped curve of riboflavin could be observed in Fig. S6d, besides, the elute order of four vitamins have made a difference when the methanol content from low shifted to high proportion. The “U turn” mobile phase composition generally depended on the polarity of the solute and the type of the stationary phase. The probability of the dual HILIC-RP mechanism increases for polar stationary phase containing a significant non-polar moiety. On the whole, the above results indicated that the retention of eight classes of analytes on the Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm column was under multiple separation modes involving RPLC and HILIC. Additionally, as shown in Fig. S12, trends scatterplot of retentions (log k) with log P also confirmed the above results of chromatographic analysis.

In 1989, Nobuo Tanaka developed a simple characterization method to evaluate the chromatographic behavior of stationary phase [37]. The selected solutes and relevant information were listed in Table S2. The retention factor of N-pentylbenzene could reflect the hydrophobic ability of chromatographic materials; methylene selectivity was based upon the separation factor α(CH2) for N-pentylbenzene and butylbenzene. In the same way, the remaining parameters (α(T/P), α1(B/P), α2(B/P)) were investigated to study the properties of hydrogen bonding interaction and the silanol activity. Also α(N/T) could represent the π-π aromatic interactions [38].

Table S3 listed Tanaka test results of two types columns. k PeB values 4.68 for The Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm column showed more hydrophobic interaction compared with 0.44 for Sil-GQDs column, that was due to the presence of dodecyl chains. α(CH2) values can also describe the hydrophobicity of stationary phase, because methylene groups were another steric factor contributing to hydrophobic interaction. In this work, the low α(CH2) values 1.49 and 1.56 indicated the both columns had a preference toward polar compounds, which was consistent with the chromatographic results of hydrophilic analytes such as nucleosides/nucleobases. A much higher αT/O values 2.5 with Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm verified the steric selectivity. According to Tanaka test, the total silanol activity and the acid activity of silanol groups were estimated by α1(B/P) and α2(B/P) at the pH 7.0 and pH 3.0. For the π-π aromatic interactions, Sil-GQDs column performed stronger than the final column based the values. The results suggested that the two columns displayed a relatively low silanol activity, possibly because bonding of GQDs and PNIPAAm on silica that had shield the silanols.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of hydrophilic ingredients in banlangen granules
ce:section.ce:para: As a traditional Chinese herbal medicine, banlangen has been used for the prevention and treatment of virus-related respiratory diseases such as influenza virus infection. Research suggested that the presence of some water-soluble compounds such as nucleosides had the potent antiviral activity via interfere in the gene expression of virus [39]. But the determination of this kinds of compounds often needed complex conditions or high cost. For this reason, it’s meaningful to develop a more simplify method to separate these compositions. In Fig. 7 , three nucleosides and nucleobases (adenosine, uridine, adenine) and phenylpropanol glycoside syringin were observed based on retention time of standards. As shown in Table S4, small deviation of retention time appeared between standards and practical sample. Furthermore, combining Fig. S13 and Fig. 8 , the results were also evaluated by HPLC/QTOF-MS, which verified the reliability of chromatographic separation and the related information was listed in Table 2 . In a nutshell, this Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm column has the promising potential for further analysis of complex Chinese medicine or other complex samples.

We firstly succeeded in reporting a dual-mode chromatographic stationary phase generated by grafting GQDs and NIPAAm onto silica through RAFT polymerization. The performance has been assessed systematically by varying the organic phase content of the mobile phase with the retention of diverse compound classes, i.e., alkylbenzenes, PAHs, biphenyls, nucleosides/nucleobases, phenols, anilines, water-soluble vitamins and amino acids. In addition, Tanaka test and association of log P and retentions (log k) were further discussed for evaluating the retention mechanism of Sil-GQDs-PNIPAAm column. All analytic results suggested that the retention of test solutes on the Sil-GQDs-PNIAAm stationary phase was carried out by mixed-mode mechanisms containing hydrophobic, hydrophilic, π-π and hydrogen bond interactions, which can be successfully used in both HILIC and RPLC applications. As a proof of the separation ability, the commercial columns were also utilized, confirming the developed mixed-mode stationary phase exhibited a wide-range and high efficiency separation performance.

Qiurong Luo, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Zhirong Zhong, Software, Visualization, Investigation. Yunchao Zheng, Resources, Writing - original draft. Die Gao, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Zhining Xia, Project administration, Supervision, Validation. Lujun Wang, Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 21804112), the Project of Department of Education of Sichuan Province (NO. 18ZA0527), the talent introduction program of Southwest Medical University (NO. 090300040040), the university level fund of Southwest Medical University (NO. 2017-ZRQN-0118) and the Sichuan Science and Technology Program (2020YFS0313).

ce:section.ce:para:
